meta.association: Autosomal dominant APOE p.Leu149del in the receptor-binding region (primary form)

evidence.reference_title: Familial splenomegaly: macrophage hypercatabolism of lipoproteins associated with apolipoprotein E mutation [apolipoprotein E (delta149 Leu)].
evidence.snippet: A mutation of apoE was demonstrated, with a 3-bp deletion resulting in the loss of a leucine at position 149 in the receptor-binding region of the apoE molecule

evidence.reference_title: Variable expressivity of the clinical and biochemical phenotype associated with the apolipoprotein E p.Leu149del mutation.
evidence.snippet: An apoE p.Leu149del mutation was found in both subjects.

nodes.description: The initiating defect is impaired macrophage handling of lipids and lipoproteins. In the best-characterized primary form, an autosomal dominant APOE mutation (p.Leu149del) in the LDL-receptor-binding region deranges lipoprotein metabolism and drives macrophage hypercatabolism of lipoproteins. In secondary forms the upstream cause is a lysosomal hydrolase deficiency (e.g., acid sphingomyelinase) or an excess substrate load (high cell turnover, dyslipidemia, intravenous lipid emulsion).
nodes.id: Defective Macrophage Lipid and Lipoprotein Handling

evidence.reference_title: Familial splenomegaly: macrophage hypercatabolism of lipoproteins associated with apolipoprotein E mutation [apolipoprotein E (delta149 Leu)].
evidence.snippet: we provide evidence linking the syndrome to an inherited dominant mutation in the apoE gene, a 3-bp deletion on the background of an apoE 3 allele that causes a derangement in lipid metabolism and leads to splenomegaly

nodes.description: Indigestible, oxidized lipid and lipoprotein material accumulates within macrophage lysosomes and polymerizes into ceroid/lipofuscin pigment. The resulting lipid-laden macrophages display the characteristic deep blue cytoplasmic granules on Wright-Giemsa staining that define the sea-blue histiocyte.
nodes.id: Lysosomal Ceroid Accumulation and Sea-Blue Pigment Formation

evidence.reference_title: Sea Blue Histiocytosis Concordant With Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura.

nodes.description: Splenomegaly is the most consistent clinical finding, reflecting accumulation of storage histiocytes in the spleen.

evidence.reference_title: Sea Blue Histiocytosis Concordant With Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura.
evidence.snippet: It may occur rarely as a primary genetic clinical syndrome characterized by splenomegaly, hypertriglyceridemia and thrombocytopenia.

nodes.description: Sea-blue histiocytes accumulate in the bone marrow, spleen, and liver (and at times lymph nodes and lung), producing organomegaly. Splenic involvement with sequestration and marrow infiltration underlies the thrombocytopenia and other cytopenias.
nodes.id: Storage Histiocyte Infiltration of Reticuloendothelial Organs

evidence.snippet: hepatomegaly and splenomegaly were identified, with PAS and Giemsa positive intracellular ceroid granular deposits

nodes.description: Thrombocytopenia, often with a hemorrhagic diathesis, is part of the classic primary syndrome and is commonly aggravated by hypersplenism.

evidence.reference_title: Sea Blue Histiocytosis Concordant With Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura.
evidence.snippet: It may occur rarely as a primary genetic clinical syndrome characterized by splenomegaly, hypertriglyceridemia and thrombocytopenia.
